title: Start Here
subtitle: What Sutton SignWriting is, why it matters, and where to go next
summary: Plain-language orientation to Sutton SignWriting, the Sutton SignWriting Foundations series, and the core public argument.
synopsis: Sutton SignWriting is a writing system for signed languages. Signed languages are full human languages, but they have often lacked durable, readable, and writable text within the language itself. The Foundations series begins from two claims: language is not sound, and writing is not only a line.
cover_image_alt: An open book of signed-writing symbols unfolding into organized paths for research, evidence, infrastructure, and technical work.

slides.title: What You Are Entering
slides.summary: Sutton SignWriting is a writing system for signed languages and the center of a broader platform of research, evidence, infrastructure, and technical work.

image_prompts.concrete: A polished academic orientation slide showing Sutton SignWriting at the center of connected publication series: foundations, research, evidence, infrastructure, formal text, and critical review
image_prompts.abstract: A central written-sign symbol radiating into organized pathways of scholarship and infrastructure

bullets: Sutton SignWriting is a writing system for signed languages
The platform has multiple publication series
Each series has a distinct job
Start Here is the map
slides.speech:
Start Here is the map into the Sutton SignWriting platform. Sutton SignWriting is a writing system for signed languages. Around that writing system is a broader platform: public essays, research-facing papers, evidence briefs, infrastructure materials, formal technical work, and critical review.

Those pieces should not be collapsed into one document. The platform is organized into series so each kind of question has a clear place.
slides.notes:
Open simply and concretely.

Do not make this sound like a marketing page. It is an orientation to a publication architecture.

Transition:
- state the central thesis that organizes the whole map.

slides.title: The Central Thesis
slides.summary: Signed languages are full human languages, and they need durable readable and writable pathways of their own.

image_prompts.concrete: Academic concept slide showing signed language, durable signed text, literacy, research, and technology connected in one restrained visual system
image_prompts.abstract: A moving visual language becoming durable written signs on a stable page

bullets: Signed languages are full human languages
Many have lacked durable written pathways
Durable text supports revision, search, teaching, research, and tools
SignWriting answers that missing layer
slides.speech:
The central idea is simple. Signed languages are full human languages, but they have often lacked equally durable readable and writable pathways of their own. That lack matters because durable text changes what a community, researcher, teacher, or software system can do with language.

Text can be revisited, revised, cited, searched, compared, taught, annotated, and processed. SignWriting matters because it offers a real written pathway for signed languages, without replacing bilingual literacy, video, or community practice.
slides.notes:
This is the one-slide thesis.

Keep the claims balanced:
- SignWriting matters
- it is not the only thing that matters
- the missing layer is durable signed text

Transition:
- define the terms that will recur.

slides.title: Three Recurring Terms
slides.summary: The platform uses durable signed text, infrastructure, and pathways in specific ways.

image_prompts.concrete: Clean glossary-style slide with three connected terms: durable signed text, infrastructure, and pathways, each shown with small visual examples
image_prompts.abstract: Three labeled conceptual pillars supporting a shared written-language platform

bullets: Durable signed text persists and can be reused
Infrastructure includes technical and human support
Pathways should be named specifically
Clear terms prevent blurred claims
slides.speech:
Three terms recur across the platform. Durable signed text means signed text that persists across time and can be revisited, revised, cited, searched, taught, and processed. Infrastructure includes technical systems such as alphabets, encodings, fonts, editors, corpora, datasets, tools, and platforms, but also human support such as teaching, stewardship, publishing practice, funding, and institutions.

Pathways should usually be named more specifically. A language pathway is not the same as a literacy pathway. A reading pathway is not the same as an expressive pathway. Keeping those terms clear keeps the claims clear.
slides.notes:
This slide prevents later confusion.

Emphasize:
- durable signed text is functional, not decorative
- infrastructure includes people and institutions
- pathway needs a modifier

Transition:
- explain why separate series are necessary.

slides.title: Why Multiple Series
slides.summary: Different audiences need different documents, and the platform works better when each kind of argument has a clear place.

image_prompts.concrete: A restrained publication architecture diagram showing one platform divided into distinct series for concept, research, writing theory, evidence, infrastructure, formal specification, and review
image_prompts.abstract: One broad river separating into clear navigable channels

bullets: Conceptual readers need a core argument
Researchers need a disciplined model
Evidence readers need documented proof points
Technical readers need mechanism
Review readers need careful evaluation
slides.speech:
The platform uses multiple series because not every audience needs the same document. A general reader needs the broad conceptual frame. A researcher needs a more disciplined developmental model. A reader asking whether SignWriting is real in practice needs evidence. A technical reader needs the formal text model. A reviewer needs careful separation among writing, notation, encoding, rendering, and infrastructure.

The series prevent one document from doing every job badly. They let each argument mature in the right place.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- multiple series are a clarity mechanism
- they prevent overburdened documents
- they make the publication easier to read

Transition:
- walk through the core platform series.

slides.title: Core Platform Series
slides.summary: The main public series move from concept to research, writing theory, evidence, stewardship, mechanism, and review.

image_prompts.concrete: A clean publication map listing Sutton SignWriting Foundations, Sutton SignWriting Language and Literacy Research, Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting, Sutton SignWriting Evidence and Adoption, Sutton SignWriting Infrastructure and Program, Formal SignWriting, and Critical Review
image_prompts.abstract: A structured platform map with seven strong series moving forward together

bullets: Sutton SignWriting Foundations: public conceptual entry
Sutton SignWriting Language and Literacy Research: pathway model and studies
Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting: plane writing and authored space
Sutton SignWriting Evidence and Adoption: documented reality
Sutton SignWriting Infrastructure and Program: stewardship and continuity
Formal SignWriting: technical text model
Critical Review: evaluative scholarship layer
slides.speech:
The core platform series are distinct but connected. Sutton SignWriting Foundations gives the public conceptual entry. Sutton SignWriting Language and Literacy Research carries the research-facing pathway model. Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting deepens the writing-systems argument. Sutton SignWriting Evidence and Adoption answers what is already documented. Sutton SignWriting Infrastructure and Program explains stewardship and continuity. Formal SignWriting gives the technical text model. The Critical Review series evaluates scholarship, standards, proposals, and recurring claims.

Together they form the public architecture of the Sutton SignWriting platform.
slides.notes:
This is the inventory slide.

Move quickly but clearly. The details come in later reading paths.

Transition:
- show the shortest reading path through the platform.

slides.title: The Clearest Spine
slides.summary: One short sequence carries a reader from the public concept to evidence, stewardship, and the formal sign-text model.

image_prompts.concrete: A publication reading path with six document covers connected by arrows: Language Without Sound, The Shape of Writing, The Four Language Pathways, Brazil, Executive Brief, and The Shape of a Sign
image_prompts.abstract: Six stepping stones crossing from concept to evidence to infrastructure and technical form

bullets: Language Without Sound
The Shape of Writing
The Four Language Pathways
Brazil as the Empirical Center
Executive Brief
The Shape of a Sign
slides.speech:
There are many valid ways through the platform, but one path is the clearest spine. Begin with Language Without Sound, which explains why language is not reducible to sound and why durable literacy pathways matter. Then read The Shape of Writing, which explains why writing is not inherently linear. Move next to The Four Language Pathways for the research-facing model.

Then the path becomes practical. Brazil as the Empirical Center shows the strongest documented adoption case. Executive Brief gives the short stewardship argument. The Shape of a Sign bridges the public writing claim into the formal technical model of sign text.
slides.notes:
Use this as the reader's memory hook.

The spine is not the only route, but it is the cleanest route.

Transition:
- name alternative reader paths.

slides.title: Reader Paths
slides.summary: Different readers can enter through the path that matches their question.

image_prompts.concrete: A decision map for readers with three clear paths: new to SignWriting, research and evidence, technical and infrastructure
image_prompts.abstract: A clear wayfinding board with multiple routes through one intellectual landscape

bullets: New readers: core idea and evidence boundary
Research and evidence: pathway model and documentation
Technical and infrastructure: formal text and stewardship
slides.speech: Start Here also works as a routing document, but it does not need to split readers into eight separate identities. Most readers need one of three routes. New readers need the shortest path from the core idea to the evidence boundary. Research and evidence readers need the pathway model, reading questions, and documented use. Technical and infrastructure readers need the formal text model and the stewardship structure that keeps signed text durable.
slides.notes:
This slide should feel practical.

Keep the choices few.

Transition:
- state what the whole platform is trying to do.

slides.title: Three Jobs At Once
slides.summary: The platform clarifies the ideas, documents the reality, and builds the technical and human infrastructure.

image_prompts.concrete: Academic slide with three connected pillars: clarify the ideas, document the reality, build the infrastructure, with SignWriting examples linking them
image_prompts.abstract: Three strong pillars supporting one written-language platform

bullets: Clarify the ideas
Document the reality
Build the technical and human infrastructure
Move from possibility to sustained practice
slides.speech:
At the broadest level, the project is doing three things at once. It clarifies the ideas: what kind of language, writing, literacy, and representation are involved. It documents the reality: where SignWriting is already used, what evidence exists, and what remains open. It builds the infrastructure: alphabets, encodings, fonts, editors, corpora, datasets, publication practice, stewardship, and institutional continuity.

The work asks what kind of writing SignWriting is, what research it makes possible, where it is already real, what technical model preserves it, and what stewardship would let it persist and grow.
slides.notes:
This is the synthesis slide.

It should make the platform feel coherent rather than sprawling.

Transition:
- close by telling the reader how to start.

slides.title: Where To Begin
slides.summary: The best first move is to read the two Sutton SignWriting Foundations anchor papers, then follow the question that matters most to you.

image_prompts.concrete: A clean closing orientation slide showing two starting documents, Language Without Sound and The Shape of Writing, branching into research, evidence, infrastructure, technical, and review paths
image_prompts.abstract: Two foundation stones becoming a network of clear future paths

bullets: Begin with Language Without Sound
Then read The Shape of Writing
Follow your question into the right series
Use Start Here as the platform map
slides.speech:
The best first move is simple. Read Language Without Sound. Then read The Shape of Writing. Those two papers give the platform its conceptual base: language does not depend on sound, and writing does not depend on the line.

After that, follow the question that matters most. Research questions move into Sutton SignWriting Language and Literacy Research. Writing-system questions move into Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting. Evidence questions move into Sutton SignWriting Evidence and Adoption. Stewardship questions move into Sutton SignWriting Infrastructure and Program. Technical questions move into Formal SignWriting. Review questions move into the Critical Review series. Start Here remains the map whenever the route becomes unclear.
slides.notes:
Close with actionable direction.

Final emphasis:
- two anchor papers first
- then route by question

Delivery note:
- this should leave the reader oriented, not overwhelmed.
